title: Course Project
text:
NoteAbout the Course Project



For the project, your team must model, simulate, and build the system and implement closed-loop control.
📂 View Project Ideas & Build Resources

title: Course Project
text:
Project Assessment and Timeline
This is a 5-week project with 3 graded milestones.



Component
Weight
Due Date
Submission




Milestone 1 — Project definition & modeling
20%
April 4
Link


Milestone 2 — Simulation & early hardware
25%
April 18



Final demonstration
25%
May 3



Final technical report
20%
May 5



Individual contribution & professionalism
10%
Ongoing





Milestone 1: Project Definition and Modeling (20%)
Submit:

Chosen project and team members
Control objective
Identified input, output, actuator, sensor, and main disturbance
Initial mathematical model with assumptions
Parts list and build plan



Milestone 2: Simulation and Early Hardware Progress (25%)
Submit:

Simulation model and controller design
Plots showing system response
Progress on hardware build
Proof that sensing and actuation are working at a basic level



Final Demonstration (25%)
You must:

Give a live demo of the built system
Show closed-loop operation
Explain what works and what does not



Final Technical Report (20%)
The report should include:

Project overview
Model derivation
Simulation results
Controller design
Hardware implementation
Experimental results
Comparison between simulation and experiment
Discussion of limitations and improvements



Individual Contribution and Professionalism (10%)
Based on:

Short individual reflection
Peer evaluation
Meeting deadlines
Contribution during demo/presentation








ImportantRemember



The resources above are starting points. Your final project must include your own modeling, simulation, hardware implementation, and closed-loop control. Use these references to understand the build process, then make the project your own.

title: Course Policy
text:
Academic integrity and collaboration






WarningWhat is allowed vs not allowed



✅ Allowed - Discussing concepts and approaches at a high level with classmates
- Using course notes and posted examples
- Studying together
❌ Not allowed - Copying any part of another student’s written solution
- Copying code, derivations, or calculations (even if you “understand it”)
- Submitting work that is not fully your own


If you collaborate, your submitted work must be written independently and reflect your own understanding.

title: Course Policy
text:
Resources



Textbooks

Main: R. Dorf & R. Bishop, Modern Control Systems, 13th ed. (2017)
Optional: Franklin, Powell, Emami-Naeini, Feedback Control of Dynamic Systems, 8th ed.




Software

MATLAB/Simulink for computational work
Python may be used for selected demos or optional simulations










TipRecommendation



If you’re rusty on Laplace transforms and differential equations, spend a little time reviewing them in Week 1–2.
The Calendar page will list the specific topics to review when needed.

title: Homework
text:
NoteSubmission



Assignments are due by 11:59 PM (Asia/Riyadh) and submitted on Gradescope.
Open Gradescope Entry code: D684VR

TipQuick checklist (Gradescope)




Submit one PDF (clear scans, high contrast).
Start each question on a new page (recommended).
Use Gradescope’s tool to map pages → questions.
If the grader cannot read it, they may request a resubmission or deduct points.
For multi-page answers, link all relevant pages to that question.



Need help submitting? See Gradescope’s student guide: How to submit variable-length assignments

title: Homework
text:
Policies






WarningLate days



You have 6 late days total, with a maximum of 2 late days per assignment.
Each late day is a 24-hour extension (e.g., Wed 11:59 PM → Thu 11:59 PM = 1 late day).
No credit is given after 48 hours past the deadline, or after late days are exhausted.




Collaboration policy and honor code
Students must work individually on all homework assignments.
You may have high-level discussions with classmates, but anything you submit must be your own work.
You may reuse small snippets of publicly available code (e.g., StackOverflow) only if properly attributed.


Generative AI tools policy
In this course, every element of class assignments must be fully prepared by the student.
The use of generative AI tools for any part of your work is not allowed and will be treated as plagiarism — unless it is explicitly permitted and announced by the instructor.


Regrade policy
Regrade requests are submitted through Gradescope. Include a clear explanation of the grading error.

Deadline: within 1 week of receiving your grade, by 11:59 PM.
We may regrade the entire assignment (your score may go up or down).

Legitimate requests - Points were added incorrectly. - The answer exists but was on a different page. - The grader claims a specific case fails, but your implementation works for that case.
Illegitimate requests - Disagreeing with the rubric / asking for more partial credit without an error. - “My solution wasn’t clear, but what I meant was correct.”

title: Project Ideas & Build Resources
text: These resources are provided to help you choose, design, and build your course project. Each project below includes example open-source builds, tutorials, assembly references, or printable part resources to help you get started. You are not required to copy these exactly, but they should help you understand the hardware, components, and build process.

title: Project Ideas & Build Resources
text:
1. Self-Balancing Two-Wheel Robot
Build and control an unstable mobile robot that balances upright using sensor feedback.
What to look for: IMU sensor (e.g., MPU6050), DC gear motors with encoders, motor driver, and a 3D-printed or laser-cut chassis. Expect a moderate build and a classic PID balancing challenge.

CALOU Self-Balancing Robot
DIY Self-Balancing Robot
Arduino Two-Wheel Self-Balancing Robot

title: Project Ideas & Build Resources
text:
2. Reaction Wheel Pendulum
Use a motor-driven reaction wheel to stabilize a pendulum in the upright position.
What to look for: A brushless or brushed DC motor with an attached flywheel, an encoder or IMU for angle sensing, and a carefully balanced mechanical arm. The control challenge involves managing angular momentum transfer.

SimpleFOC Reaction Wheel Inverted Pendulum
askuric Inverted Inertia Pendulum
Ethan Lawlor RWIP

title: Project Ideas & Build Resources
text:
3. Ball-and-Beam System
Control beam angle to regulate the position of a rolling ball.
What to look for: A servo motor to tilt the beam, an ultrasonic or IR distance sensor for ball position, and a simple mechanical frame. This is a straightforward build with a well-known control problem.

Ian Carey Ball and Beam Kit
Open-Source Ball-and-Beam Platform
Dorsa-amiri Ball-and-Beam

title: Project Ideas & Build Resources
text:
4. 1-DOF Helicopter / Twin-Rotor Arm
Control the angular position of a rotor-driven arm and track reference commands.
What to look for: One or two brushless motors (propellers), a pivot-mounted arm, and an angle sensor (encoder or potentiometer). The build requires secure mounting, and the control challenge involves managing thrust-to-angle dynamics.

Van Hunter Adams 1-DoF Helicopter
Nolan Tsuchiya 1-DOF Helicopter
NabilHassan 1DOF-Copter-Backstepping

title: Project Ideas & Build Resources
text:
5. Single-Axis Gimbal Stabilization
Design a stabilization system that keeps one axis steady despite base disturbances.
What to look for: A servo or brushless motor, an IMU (e.g., MPU6050) for orientation sensing, and a gimbal frame (3D-printed or assembled from hardware). The control challenge is rejecting disturbances in real time.

DIY Arduino Gimbal — Self-Stabilizing Platform
Arduino Gimbal by The Noob Inventor
MPU6050 1-Axis Gimbal
Single-Axis Gimbal Code

title: Project Ideas & Build Resources
text:
6. Solar-Powered DC Bus Regulation
Control a DC-DC converter to regulate voltage in a small solar-energy system under changing conditions.
What to look for: A buck or boost converter circuit, voltage/current sensors, a solar panel (small), and a microcontroller for PWM-based control. This project is more circuit-focused; expect breadboard or PCB work and a power electronics control challenge.

DC-DC Buck Converter with Arduino Uno
Arduino DC-DC Buck Converter with PID Control
MPPT Buck Converter ACS712
Libre Solar MPPT 2420 HC








ImportantRemember



The resources above are starting points. Your final project must include your own modeling, simulation, hardware implementation, and closed-loop control. Use these references to understand the build process, then make the project your own.
